sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Mr. James, a 64-year-old male, presents with a 5-month history of constant pain in his left shoulder and elbow, which began after a fall 5 months ago while going up the stairs.
He reports the pain is present all the time and can be severe at times.
He has been trying to continue his exercise routine, but the pain has been exacerbated by lifting weights, and he has had to stop lifting more than 15-20 pounds.
He reports no numbness or tingling in the affected areas.
Past medical history: [NOT MENTIONED]
Review of systems: [NOT MENTIONED]
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The patient's left shoulder and elbow were tender to palpation, with no warmth, erythema, or deformity.
The Hawkins-Kennedy and Neer's test were positive.
The patient had normal proximal and distal upper extremity strength, intact median and radial ulnar sensation, and abduction to 90 degrees.
The empty can test was normal.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has impingement syndrome of the left shoulder.
Problem list: Shoulder impingement syndrome; wrist pain due to shoulder impingement (status: suspected).
Plan:
I prescribed physical therapy with Dr. Martha Rivera two days per week to address the patient's impingement syndrome.
The patient will also continue to ice the shoulder and rest as much as possible.
The patient will be referred for physical therapy within the next day or two, and they will contact the office if their symptoms worsen or do not improve.
Follow-up: The patient will be followed up with Dr. Isabella to assess the effectiveness of the physical therapy and to determine if further treatment is needed.
